# 4 Types of Quantum Dot Displays:  QLED vs QDEL vs QD-OLED vs QD-micro LED

![4 Types of Quantum Dot Displays:  QLED vs QDEL vs QD-OLED vs QD-micro LED ](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201280%20720'%3E%3C/svg%3E "4 Types of Quantum Dot Displays:  QLED vs QDEL vs QD-OLED vs QD-micro LED ")
![4 Types of Quantum Dot Displays:  QLED vs QDEL vs QD-OLED vs QD-micro LED ](https://static.digit.in/COVER.png "4 Types of Quantum Dot Displays:  QLED vs QDEL vs QD-OLED vs QD-micro LED ")
![Deepak Singh](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%2070%2070'%3E%3C/svg%3E)![Deepak Singh](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/2d17028378e6a1452a3bd13ce0cc3d40?s=70&d=mm&r=g)

![Deepak Singh](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/2d17028378e6a1452a3bd13ce0cc3d40?s=70&d=mm&r=g)

Quantum Dots have undeniably become the cornerstone of modern TV innovation. These high-efficiency colour converters are being used to enhance all sorts of display technologies including LCD, OLED and even micro LED.  Despite their growing presence, there’s often confusion about the different types of Quantum Dot-based technologies and how they differ. Let’s break down the most popular types and highlight their differences.

## **What are quantum dots?**

Quantum dots are tiny semiconductor particles that emit light when excited. What colour of light they emit depends on the size of the quantum dot. The important thing is that there is no loss of efficiency (in a way, brightness) when colour conversion takes place via quantum dots. In comparison, when colour filters are used a significant portion of light is absorbed resulting in loss of quantum efficiency.

**Also Read:** [8 Different Types of OLED Display Technologies You Should Know About](https://www.digit.in/features/general/8-different-types-of-oled-displays-that-you-should-know-about-rgb-to-pholed-8-innovative-oled-displa-68657.html)

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfprrKbO_Y3AwNrkP1ui2CrQwjbQiPQrQmE1XlsXxx1faDRhhrK0EJD7ChWCchUb2TopSsNnJFcp6QZXo2JRQ2n_QZJoWo-3QSm7nX8p3JJTITluDmRlLIQPbi9b0_XJh2vfjzQlg?key=Sx9F3iNB0fDDYDbeRH-EgXzu)

Source: Samsung

Quantum dots can be classified into several types based on the material used and applications. But there are two popular types that you need to be familiar with.

Electroluminescent Quantum Dots (EL-QDs) emit light when excited by an electric current. The more popular ones are Photoluminescent Quantum Dots (PL-QDs) which emit light when excited by UV light or blue light.

## QLED Displays

QLEDs are the most popular type of Quantum Dots-based displays in consumer devices today.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdBDN_w0wZQazi7UVvpS41cgZLgumdHQkgJtTxpmuJ2z3DMoU1j3RIpw8ALCrKzFtQYKp4129pbulENMOqBWkbtb10TcpLp7RBuDyjkFns8hMk7m1oVi-y7UfQd0pt5x-nVhnve?key=Sx9F3iNB0fDDYDbeRH-EgXzu)

Source: Nanosys

These displays use a quantum dot enhancement layer (either QDEF film or xQDEF diffuser plate) between the backlight and LCD panel.

The blue light from the backlight LEDs falls on Quantum Dots and some of it is converted to Green and Red. This results in a healthy mix of Red, Green and Blue components in the white light which is necessary to achieve a larger colour volume.

**Also Check:** [QNED vs QLED vs OLED TVs: What’s the difference?](https://www.digit.in/features/tvs/qned-vs-qled-vs-oled-whats-the-difference.html)

QLED TVs are easy to manufacture which is also why they are quite popular. LG’s QNED TVs and some of Sony’s Triluminous TVs are also based on the same technology. The MacBook Pro launched in 2024 also uses QDEF film in its display for the first time as was discovered by [DSCC](https://www.displaysupplychain.com/).

## QD-OLED Displays

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXeS3swS1Uu3fPwD-tEv3IeC7Gk5LP5-ko09ldUzoTgjA2_DxS_ba2PH7WhMksbAoR95KrSAqeBnJJFGO8CItuXmzeVlMGPLzIOdVlFEtwwTYQHlaW2rcCfLzDbQbcyMl2bLTTvT?key=Sx9F3iNB0fDDYDbeRH-EgXzu)

QD-OLED uses a Quantum Dots layer for colour conversion, unlike WHite OLEDs which use a colour filter. It’s easy to manufacture OLED displays with red, blue and green subpixels for smartphones but not for larger display sizes like TVs and monitors.

Instead, manufacturers use a single colour emitter and then achieve colour conversion using a colour filter or quantum dots. When Quantum Dots are used, there is no loss in quantum efficiency and the display can achieve larger colour volume and higher contrast. This also enables QD-OLED TVs to be brighter than conventional white OLED TVs.

## QD-micro LED Displays

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXe5EWTSZ3-AVzTgB1wPlHWEwALcS0cRwCQPzrKPCQjoOMqf_mM1-u0jblwXwumky6-zHiJPxhQiOJBHa_IvFDnzYpsL9ftI0I6f5FymtBkDppvMMcaho6Y9cwQehDhw8nKLCDTNxw?key=Sx9F3iNB0fDDYDbeRH-EgXzu)

Source: Applied Materials

QD-micro LED displays are principally similar to the QD-OLED displays. The difference is that instead of organic emitters or OLEDs, these displays use inorganic emitters or micro LEDs which can last longer and can go much brighter.

Again, it’s challenging to transfer three different colour micro LEDs to a substrate and thus manufacturers see using a single blue colour emitter followed by a Quantum Dot colour conversion layer in later stages as a more pragmatic approach. QD-micro LED displays are still a few years away from consumer devices.

## QDEL or QD-LED or Nano LED Display

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20370'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://static.digit.in/qd-el-2-1024x370.jpg)

QDEL is one of the most promising upcoming display technologies. It is also referred to as Nano LED or QD-LED. In fact, there were times when this technology was also referred to as QNED and QLED, which were later registered by LG and Samsung for different display technologies and are now quite popular.

All the other applications that we have discussed above use Photoluminescent Quantum Dots that are excited using blue light. QD-EL or Nano LEDs on the other hand use Electroluminescent Quantum Dots which like regular LEDs can emit light when excited by current. In QDEL displays, Quantum dots do everything on their own. They don’t require an OLED or LED layer and thus offer high purity and brightness.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfgkvkqh1RyeFqEitPIGbTRBp6EwelEaSzKYsOgi-OMErnOYEKm1xrMtTKTKPgFpYQYKHWG5Ty_rk0iETmcr4dJPjDAvPSS77AEOEaUXC7Q3W9tCm0K0DVAMXACGpgy1RxG79Lv6A?key=Sx9F3iNB0fDDYDbeRH-EgXzu)

The technology was the prime focus at SID Display Week and TCL also showcased a 14-inch 2.8K QD-EL laptop screen prototype fabricated by ink-jet printing. This display offers a variable refresh rate (30 Hz – 120 Hz) and a wide viewing angle.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of display device

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/99/QD_S.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:QD_S.jpg)

Colloidal quantum dots irradiated with a UV light. Different sized quantum dots emit different color light due to quantum confinement.

A **quantum dot display** is a [display device](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Display_device "Display device") that uses [quantum dots](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dots "Quantum dots") (QD), semiconductor nanocrystals which can produce pure monochromatic[[a]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-1) red, green, and blue light. _Photo-emissive_ quantum dot particles are used in LCD backlights or display color filters. Quantum dots are excited by the blue light from the display panel to emit pure basic colors, which reduces light losses and color crosstalk in color filters, improving display brightness and [color gamut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Color_gamut "Color gamut"). Light travels through QD layer film and traditional RGB filters made from color pigments, or through QD filters with red/green QD color converters and blue passthrough. Although the QD color filter technology is primarily used in [LED-backlit LCDs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LED-backlit_LCD "LED-backlit LCD"), it is applicable to other display technologies which use color filters, such as blue/UV [active-matrix organic light-emitting diode](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Active-matrix_organic_light-emitting_diode "Active-matrix organic light-emitting diode") (AMOLED) or [QNED](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#QNED)/[MicroLED](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MicroLED "MicroLED") display panels.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-2)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-3)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-4) LED-backlit LCDs are the main application of photo-emissive quantum dots, though blue organic light-emitting diode ([OLED](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OLED "OLED")) panels with QD color filters are now coming to market.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-5)

_Electro-emissive_ or _electroluminescent_ quantum dot displays are an experimental type of display based on quantum-dot light-emitting diodes (QD-LED; also EL-QLED, ELQD, QDEL). These displays are similar to AMOLED and MicroLED displays, in that light would be produced directly in each pixel by applying electric current to inorganic nano-particles. Manufacturers asserted that QD-LED displays could support large, flexible displays and would not degrade as readily as OLEDs, making them good candidates for [flat-panel TV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flat-panel_TV "Flat-panel TV") screens, [digital cameras](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera"), [mobile phones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") and [handheld game consoles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-New_Scientist-6)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-Evident_Technologies_sub-7)[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-8)

As of June 2016,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Quantum_dot_display&action=edit) all commercial products, such as LCD TVs branded as _QLED_, employ quantum dots as _photo-emissive_ particles; _electro-emissive_ QD-LED TVs exist in laboratories only.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-9)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-10)

In 2023, quantum dot technology was introduced into the commercial _Mini/MicroLED_ display market, with pixel pitches of approximately 1.25 mm. By replacing conventional [AlInGaP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AlInGaP "AlInGaP")-based red light-emitting chips—which differ in material composition from green and blue [InGaN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/InGaN "InGaN") chips—with quantum dot-converted red subpixels, Quantum Dot Chip-on-Board (QD-COB) displays demonstrated improved color consistency across a range of viewing angles.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-11)[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-12)

Quantum dot displays are capable of displaying wider color gamuts, with some devices approaching full coverage of the [BT.2020](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BT.2020 "BT.2020") color gamut.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-SID2019tp-13)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-creol_rec2020-14)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-bul3-15) QD-OLED and QD-LED displays can achieve the same contrast as OLED/MicroLED displays with "perfect" black levels in the off state, unlike LED-backlit LCDs.

By the early 2020s, quantum dot (QD) color conversion began to be applied in _MicroLED microdisplays_ to achieve full-color output.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-16)[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-17)_MicroLED microdisplays_—commonly used in near-eye devices such as [augmented reality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") (AR) glasses and micro projectors—typically measure under 0.3 inches in diagonal and feature pixel pitches below 10 μm. At this scale, conventional mass transfer of discrete red, green, and blue microLEDs is technically challenging and cost-prohibitive. Instead, full color is achieved by starting with a blue microLED array and applying quantum dot layers to down-convert portions of the emission to red and green. Two main QD color conversion technologies have emerged: one embeds quantum dots in nanoporous GaN on blue LEDs (e.g., Nanopore Quantum Dot, or NPQD), and the other uses patterned quantum dot [photoresist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photoresist "Photoresist") layers over the microLED array. [[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-18)[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-19) These approaches enable extremely high pixel densities and sufficient brightness for compact full-color displays—for example, QD [photoresist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photoresist "Photoresist") has been used in a 0.22-inch display at over 7,000 PPI, reaching brightness levels above 150,000 nits. Additional experimental methods, such as inkjet printing of QD inks, are also under investigation for micron-scale integration.

Working principle
-----------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Quantum_dot_display&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Working principle")]

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Samsung_QLED_TV_8K_-_75_inches_-_2018-11-02.jpg/250px-Samsung_QLED_TV_8K_-_75_inches_-_2018-11-02.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Samsung_QLED_TV_8K_-_75_inches_-_2018-11-02.jpg)

Samsung QLED TV 8K - 75 inches

The idea of using quantum dots as a light source emerged in the 1990s. Early applications included imaging using QD infrared photodetectors, [light emitting diodes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light_emitting_diode "Light emitting diode") and single-color light emitting devices.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-20) Starting in the early 2000s, scientists started to realize the potential of developing quantum dots for light sources and displays.[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-bul-21)

QDs are either _photo-emissive_ ([photoluminescent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photoluminescent "Photoluminescent")) or _electro-emissive_ ([electroluminescent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electroluminescent "Electroluminescent")) allowing them to be readily incorporated into new emissive display architectures.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-Nanoco_Technologies_sub-22) Quantum dots naturally produce monochromatic light, so they are more efficient than white light sources when color filtered and allow more saturated colors that reach nearly 100% of [Rec. 2020](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rec._2020 "Rec. 2020") color gamut.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-creol_rec2020-14)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-bul3-15)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-SID2019tp-13)

### Quantum dot enhancement layer

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Quantum_dot_display&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Quantum dot enhancement layer")]

A widespread practical application is using quantum dot enhancement film (QDEF) layer to improve the [LED backlighting in LCD TVs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LED-backlit_LCD "LED-backlit LCD"). Light from a [blue LED](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light-emitting_diode#Blue_and_ultraviolet "Light-emitting diode") backlight is converted by QDs to relatively pure red and green, so that this combination of blue, green and red light incurs less blue-green crosstalk and light absorption in the color filters after the LCD screen, thereby increasing useful light throughput and providing a better [color gamut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Color_gamut "Color gamut").

The first manufacturer shipping TVs of this kind was [Sony](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sony "Sony") in 2013 as _Triluminos_, Sony's trademark for the technology.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-23) At the [Consumer Electronics Show](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consumer_Electronics_Show "Consumer Electronics Show") 2015, [Samsung Electronics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics"), [TCL Corporation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TCL_Corporation "TCL Corporation") and Sony showed QD-enhanced LED-backlighting of LCD TVs.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-24)[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-25)[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-26) At the CES 2017, Samsung rebranded their 'SUHD' TVs as 'QLED'; later in April 2017, Samsung formed the QLED Alliance with [Hisense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hisense "Hisense") and [TCL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TCL_Corporation "TCL Corporation") to produce and market QD-enhanced TVs.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-qled_alliance-27)[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-28)

Quantum dot on glass (QDOG) replaces QD film with a thin QD layer coated on top of the light-guide plate (LGP), reducing costs and improving efficiency.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-Nanosys_TFUG-29)[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-DSCC_QDOG-30)

Traditional white LED backlights that use blue LEDs with on-chip or on-rail red-green QD structures are being researched, though high operating temperatures negatively affect their lifespan.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-pid_samsungdisplay-31)[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-hdtvmagazine_2017-32)

### Quantum dot color converter

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Quantum_dot_display&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Quantum dot color converter")]

QD color converter (QDCC) LED-backlit LCDs would use QD film or ink-printed QD layer with red/green sub-pixel patterned (i.e. aligned to precisely match the red and green subpixels) quantum dots to produce pure red/green light; blue subpixels can be transparent to pass through the pure blue LED backlight, or can be made with blue patterned quantum dots in case of UV-LED backlight. This configuration effectively replaces passive color filters, which incur substantial losses by filtering out 2/3 of passing light, with photo-emissive QD structures, improving power efficiency and/or peak brightness, and enhancing color purity.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-pid_samsungdisplay-31)[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-creol_qd_2017-33)[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-34) Because quantum dots depolarize the light, output polarizer (the analyzer) needs to be moved behind the color converter and embedded in-cell of the LCD glass; this would improve viewing angles as well. In-cell arrangement of the analyzer and/or the polarizer would also reduce depolarization effects in the LC layer, increasing contrast ratio. To reduce self-excitement of QD film and to improve efficiency, the ambient light can be blocked using traditional color filters, and reflective polarizers can direct light from the QDCC towards the viewer. As only blue or UV light passes through the liquid crystal layer, it can be made thinner, resulting in faster [pixel response times](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Response_time_(technology) "Response time (technology)").[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-creol_qd_2017-33)[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-creol_depol_2017-35)

[Nanosys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nanosys "Nanosys") made presentations of their photo-emissive color converter technology during 2017; commercial products were expected by 2019, though in-cell polarizer remained a major challenge.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-36)[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-qled_alliance-27)[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-37)[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-38)[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-39)[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-nanosys_inkjet_2017-40)[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-qd_forum_2018-41)[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-qd_future_2018-42)[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-nanosys_ces2018-43) As of December 2019, issues with in-cell polarizer remain unresolved and no LCDs with QD color converter appeared on the market since then.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-44)

Saphlux, Inc. developed a nanopore quantum dot (NPQD) color conversion method for microLEDs. The NPQD process creates an in-situ nanoporous layer on a GaN microLED wafer, which is filled with quantum dots to convert blue emission into red or green light, enabling monolithic full-color displays with improved brightness, efficiency, and reliability.[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-45) In 2024, Saphlux launched a QD-COB (quantum dot chip-on-board) Mini/MicroLED display using NPQD red chips to replace conventional AlInGaP LEDs, achieving better color consistency, wider viewing angles, and arsenic-free design.[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-46)[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-47) The same technology has been applied to AR-focused MicroLED microdisplays, including a 0.39-inch full-color prototype shown in August 2023 and a 0.13-inch version targeting pilot production in 2025.[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-48) These developments aim to address key challenges in full-color microLED fabrication for both large displays and compact AR systems.

QD color converters can be used with OLED or micro-LED panels, improving their efficiency and color gamut.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-Nanosys_TFUG-29)[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-nanosys_ces2018-43)[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-Samsung_QD-OLED-49)[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-etnews-QD-OLED-50) QD-OLED panels with blue emitters and red-green color converters are researched by Samsung and TCL; as of May 2019, Samsung intends to start production in 2021.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-Samsung_IMID2018-51)[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-52)[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-53)[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-54)[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-55)[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-56) In October 2019, Samsung Display announced an investment of $10.8 billion in both research and production, with the aim to convert all their 8G panel factories to QD-OLED production during 2019–2025.[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-57)[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-58)[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-59)[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-beyond_qd-oled-60) Samsung Display presented 55" and 65" QD-OLED panels at [CES 2022](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consumer_Electronics_Show#2022 "Consumer Electronics Show"), with TVs from Samsung Electronics and Sony to be released later in 2022.[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-61) QD-OLED displays show better color volume, covering 90% of Rec.2020 color gamut with peak brightness of 1500 nits, while current OLED and LCD TVs cover 70–75% of Rec.2020 (95–100% of DCI-P3).[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-TheVerge_QD-OLED_gamut-62)[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-SGS_QD-Display-63)[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-RTings_Rec2020_gamut-64)

#### QNED

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Quantum_dot_display&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: QNED")]

A further development of QD-OLED displays is quantum dot nanorod emitting diode (QNED) display[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-sammobile-qned2021-65) which replaces blue OLED layer with [InGaN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/InGaN "InGaN")/[GaN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GaN "GaN") blue [nanorod](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nanorod "Nanorod") LEDs. Nanorods have a larger emitting surface compared to planar LED, allowing increased efficiency and higher light emission. Nanorod solution is ink-printed on the substrate, then subpixels are aligned in-place by electric current, and QD color converters are placed on top of red/green subpixels.[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-66)[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-67) Samsung Display was expected to begin test production of QNED panels in 2021,[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-sammobils-qned-68)[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-sammobile-qned2021-65) with mass production in 2024–2025, but test production has been postponed as of May 2022.[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-69)[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-70)

Starting in 2021 LG Electronics introduced a series of TVs branded as "QNED Mini LED". These TVs are based on [LCD displays](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liquid-crystal_display "Liquid-crystal display") with mini LED backlighting and don't use self-emissive technologies.[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_dot_display#cite_note-71) LG explains QNED is LG's premium LCD TV brand with LG's proprietary wide color gamut technology. It enables light from the backlight to be expressed in pure colors that are as realistic as they appear to the eye in general life.

Quantum dots have attracted attention as next-generation material for a wide range of applications including displays, medical devices and solar cells. In 2014, Samsung Electronics developed the world’s first no-cadmium quantum dot material and successfully commercialized quantum dot technology with its SUHD TVs. Since 2017, the company has continued to build on its legacy of quantum dot mastery through QLED — its own quantum dot TV series. Samsung Newsroom explored how quantum dots are taking Samsung displays to the next level.

### 

### **Quantum Dots: The Next Generation of Display Innovation**

Quantum dots are ultra-fine semiconductor particles that are tens of thousands of times thinner than a human hair. Since inception, their physical characteristics that allow them to provide the highest level of color accuracy and brightness among existing materials had them positioned to revolutionize display technology.

When used in displays, quantum dots support a wide color gamut that closely matches colors perceived by the human eye and facilitate pixel-level light adjustment for more accurate black levels. Emitting light in all directions, quantum dots deliver uniform luminance and consistent color from any viewing angle while minimizing blue light exposure for a more comfortable viewing experience.

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main1.jpg)

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main1.jpg)

▲ SUHD TVs at CES 2015

### **What Sets Quantum Dot TVs Apart: Content, Film Quality and No-Cadmium Technology**

The TV industry continues research and development into the commercialization of quantum dots as the material becomes a game-changer in display technology. For that reason, a variety of quantum dot TVs have hit the market recently — offering a wide range of options to customers.

However, key differences in quantum dot TVs lie in how the technology is implemented and the overall quality of the display. To ensure a premium viewing experience, factors such as the amount of quantum dot content, the quality of quantum dot film and the use of no-cadmium materials must be considered.

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main2F.jpg)

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main2F.jpg)

▲ Factors to consider when selecting a high-quality quantum dot TV

### **Quantum Dot Content**

The true quality of a quantum dot TV is defined by its quantum dot content. The quantum dot film requires a minimum of 3,000 parts per million (ppm) of the material to achieve the vivid, rich picture quality and color expression that only quantum dots can deliver.

### **Quantum Dot Film**

Samsung QLEDs eliminate the need for a separate phosphor layer, enhancing light and energy efficiency while delivering more vivid colors. A quantum dot OLED (QD-OLED), which consists of a thin-film transistor (TFT) layer,1 a self-emitting light source and a quantum dot film that uses the light emitted from the light source, takes a step further enhancing picture quality. In either case, a dedicated quantum dot film that contains sufficient quantum dots is key in delivering top-class picture quality and longevity.

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main3F.jpg)

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main3F.jpg)

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main3-1.jpg)

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main3-1.jpg)

▲ A comparison of QD-OLED and LCD displays

### **No Cadmium**

In the early stages of developing quantum dot TVs, cadmium was essential to achieving the key benefits of quantum dots such as color reproduction and contrast ratio. At the time, cadmium was considered the most efficient material for producing quantum dots.

However, cadmium’s toxicity became a significant obstacle to the commercialization of quantum dot technology. The element posed serious threats to the environment — making its widespread use difficult despite being the most suitable material for implementing quantum dot technology.

In response to this challenge, Samsung developed the world’s first no-cadmium quantum dot material in 2014 and successfully commercialized quantum dot technology with its SUHD TVs in the following year to open a new era of quantum dot TVs.

### **10 Years of Quantum Dot Innovation and Leadership**

Samsung has quickly recognized the potential of quantum dot technology and led innovation in the global display market over the past decade through continuous research and investment.

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main4.jpg)

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main4.jpg)

▲ A timeline of Samsung’s quantum dot technology development from 2001 to 2022

Samsung began researching and developing quantum dot technology in 2001 — at a time when there was limited research on non-cadmium materials. Achieving vivid colors required making the nano-sized particles uniform, but the lack of technology and research made mass production extremely challenging.

Despite these obstacles, Samsung succeeded in creating a no-cadmium nanocrystal material in 2014. Since then, the company has accumulated extensive expertise — registering more than 150 patents — and continuously worked on advancing the technology. Samsung’s long-standing commitment culminated in 2015 when the company unveiled the world’s first SUHD TVs with no-cadmium quantum dot technology.

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main5.jpg)

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main5.jpg)

▲ QLED TVs (75Q8C and 88Q8F) at Samsung’s First Look 2017 event during CES 2017

Samsung’s QLED lineup was revealed in 2017, setting a new standard for premium TVs that overcame the limitations of OLED TVs. By applying metal quantum dot technology, Samsung achieved the Digital Cinema Initiative’s color standard DCI-P3 and achieved 100% color volume for the first time in the world — thereby presenting unparalleled color expression. Notably, the use of inorganic quantum dot technology protected the screens from burn-in2 to ensure consistent picture quality over time.

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main6.jpg)

![](https://img.global.news.samsung.com/global/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Samsung-TVs-and-Displays-Quantum-Dot-TVs-Real-Quantum-Dot-Guide_main6.jpg)

▲ (From left to right) Kwang-Hee Kim, Dr. Taehyung Kim, Dr, Eunjoo Jang, Sungwoo Kim and Seon-Myeong Choi from Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology

Following its [success](https://news.samsung.com/global/samsung-fellows-study-on-the-potential-commercialization-of-qleds-published-in-leading-science-journal-nature) in developing a red light-emitting element for displays in 2019, the company enhanced the luminous efficiency of blue self-emitting QLEDs — considered the most challenging to implement among the three primary QLED colors3 — to an industry-leading 20.2%.

“Discovering a blue material for self-emitting QLEDs and demonstrating industry-leading performance at the device level were significant achievements of this research,” said Dr. Eunjoo Chang, a fellow at Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology. “Samsung’s distinctive quantum dot technology has once again overcome technical barriers.”

This cutting-edge advancements led to the launch of the QD-OLED TVs, making history at CES 2022 by winning the Best of Innovation award for integrating quantum dot technology and OLED displays.

Samsung remains dedicated to advancing quantum dot technology through continuous innovation. The company continues to invest in leading display technology — from SUHD to Neo QLED — by offering high brightness, color accuracy and frequency. Driven by Samsung’s unrivaled quantum dot innovations, the future of display technology is brighter than ever.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Quantum Dot Liquid Crystal Displays (QD-LCDs) utilize a quantum dot enhancement film to improve color accuracy and brightness by converting blue LED backlight into pure red and green light.
Quantum Dot Light Emitting Diodes (QLEDs) feature a quantum dot layer that enhances color reproduction and brightness, utilizing LED backlighting to produce a vibrant viewing experience.

Quantum Dot Organic Light Emitting Diodes (QD-OLEDs) combine quantum dots with OLED technology, allowing for superior color volume and contrast without the efficiency losses of traditional color filters.
QD-LCDs excel in brightness, QLEDs offer excellent color volume, while QD-OLEDs provide the best contrast. Each technology utilizes quantum dots to enhance overall display performance.
